title: The Longest Increasing Subsequence Problem revisited
abstract: The Longest Increasing Subsequence problem - a classic combinatorial challenge with deep connections to statistical mechanics- exhibits a rich thermodynamic landscape. Introducing a temperature we identify two distinct energy scales: A Schottky-like crossover at T_cross and a condensation transition at T_cond, below which the number of maximum-length configurations becomes sub-exponential in system size. We also show that despite the existence of polynomial-time dynamic programming algorithms for the ground state, local Monte Carlo dynamics, after sudden quenches at low temperatures, become trapped in metastable state configurations displaying characteristic glassy signatures: two-step relaxation, persistent dynamical overlaps and aging. On the other hand, logarithmic annealing tracks equilibrium down to the ground state. These results establish that thermodynamic sparsity - not energetic barriers - can render local search dynamically intractable, positioning the LIS problem as a bridge between exactly solvable optimization and glassy spin-glass phenomenology.
summary: This paper analyzes the Longest Increasing Subsequence problem by mapping it onto a statistical mechanical model exhibiting phase transitions. It identifies a condensation transition that causes the system's ground state manifold to become thermodynamically sparse. This sparsity is shown to be the root cause of dynamic trapping, making the problem computationally hard for local search methods, even though polynomial-time solutions exist.
main_problem: The paper revisits the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem, treating it as a system whose thermodynamic properties and dynamical behavior can be analyzed using statistical mechanics.
main_result: The LIS problem exhibits a condensation transition at a finite temperature, suggesting that thermodynamic sparsity, rather than simple energetic barriers, causes local search algorithms to become dynamically trapped in glassy states.
method: The authors employ thermodynamic formalism (microcanonical/canonical ensembles) alongside Monte Carlo simulations to study the system's phase transitions and dynamics.
model_or_system: The LIS problem is mapped onto a system defined on a sequence of random numbers, modeled as a directed polymer on a random graph (Ulam random graph). The energy is related to the length of the subsequence.
key_observables: Specific heat ($C_v$), configurational entropy ($S(\ell)$), and dynamical overlap ($Q(t)$).
important_parameters: Two critical temperatures: a Schottky crossover ($T_{	ext{cross}} \approx 0.38$) and a condensation transition ($T_{	ext{cond}} = 0.10 \pm 0.02$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the quenched sequence is i.i.d., and the low-energy sector can be modeled by independent two-level systems.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the specific heat peak at $T_{	ext{cross}}$ and the condensation transition at $T_{	ext{cond}}$, alongside entropy density convergence to a plateau below $T_{	ext{cond}}$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the thermodynamic framework, identifying two distinct temperature scales ($T_{	ext{cross}}$ and $T_{	ext{cond}}$), and then contrasting the dynamics observed during sudden quenches (glassy trapping) versus controlled annealing.
why_interesting: The connection between a combinatorial optimization problem (LIS) and glassy dynamics, driven by thermodynamic sparsity, provides a powerful analogy for understanding computational hardness in complex physical systems, relevant to disordered models studied in AMO and condensed matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Specific heat as a function of temperature for different system sizes N = 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384. The peak at Tcross ∼0.38 indicates a finite-size crossover, while the behavior at lower temperatures reveals a freezing transition at Tcond = 0.10 ± 0.02.

FIG. 3. The specific heat cv(T, N) scaled by cv(N)max for different system sizes N = 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384. The arrow indi- cates the condensation transition. In the inset we reproduce the same plot on logarithmic scale to show the exponential decay at Tcond ≈0.1.

FIG. 4. The ground state and the first excited state tem- perature T0 and T1 respectively as a function of 1/N 1/4. A linear fit indicates that both temperatures converge to Tcond = 0.10 ± 0.02 for N →∞, signaling a freezing tran- sition in the thermodynamic limit.
FIG. 5. Main panel: entropy density s(T) = S(T)/ √
FIG. 7. Energy versus time for Monte Carlo simulations at various temperatures, N = 4096. The dramatic slowing down below Tcross is indicative of the appearance of a complex land- scape.
